interpretation_summary: The replication executed a hierarchical OLS regression on a new but similar longitudinal dataset of preschool teachers, testing whether weekend relaxation (T2) predicts end-of-week joviality (T3) controlling for T1 joviality, other recovery experiences, hassles, and demographics. Execution required environment fixes and used the provided processed dataset. Results showed a positive standardized coefficient for relaxation (≈0.098) with two-tailed p≈0.085, which meets the preregistered one-tailed criterion (alpha=0.05). The effect is directionally consistent with the original (β≈0.15, p<0.01), though attenuated in magnitude and weaker in significance.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment: The executed code fit the preregistered hierarchical OLS model with the specified outcome (T3 joviality), focal predictor (req_relax), controls (age, gender, children, work hours, work days), baseline T1 joviality, other weekend experiences (control, mastery, detachment), and hassles. Listwise deletion was used. Both unstandardized and standardized (z-score) specifications were reported as planned.

deviations.issue_description: Data preparation step was skipped at runtime because a processed dataset was already available; attempts to regenerate from raw encountered path and intermediate variable issues.
deviations.impact: Low: The analysis used the intended processed dataset with required variables; estimates are unaffected.

deviations.issue_description: Runtime environment fixes (pinning package versions; auto-installing statsmodels within the container; adding site-packages to sys.path).
deviations.impact: Low: Computational environment changes do not affect the statistical model or estimates.

deviations.issue_description: Replication sample differs from original (larger, 2021 cohort, country not explicitly stated), though instruments and timing are the same.
deviations.impact: Moderate on comparability of effect size: Direction replicated, but magnitude attenuated relative to the original.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Among preschool teachers, weekend relaxation at Time 2 (Sunday night) positively predicts joviality at Time 3 (following Friday) when controlling for T1 joviality, other weekend recovery experiences (detachment, mastery, control), non-work hassles, and demographics.
results_comparison.original_results: Standardized coefficient for relaxation predicting T3 joviality: β ≈ 0.15, p < 0.01 (positive).
results_comparison.replication_results: Standardized coefficient for relaxation predicting T3 joviality: β = 0.0982, two-tailed p = 0.0851 (meets preregistered one-tailed alpha=0.05). Unstandardized coef = 0.0717. Model R^2 ≈ 0.393; Adj. R^2 ≈ 0.373; N = 337.
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes. The replication satisfies the preregistered criterion for the focal claim (positive sign and one-tailed p < 0.05, operationalized as two-tailed p < 0.10), though the effect is smaller than in the original.

replication_report: Replication successful: Weekend relaxation positively predicts end-of-week joviality with a smaller standardized effect (≈0.10) and one-tailed significance, consistent in direction with the original (β≈0.15, p<0.01).

failure_handling.suggestions: Pin Python package versions in the Dockerfile or requirements.txt to avoid shell parsing of version ranges; include statsmodels in the base image; avoid runtime installs by building the image with all dependencies.

Standardize data paths and avoid spaces in directory names (e.g., 'Raw data'); implement robust path resolution and existence checks; provide a verified data-prep pipeline or confirm the processed dataset’s provenance to ensure full reproducibility from raw.

Ensure outputs are consistently written to a documented path (e.g., /app/data/replication_data/k17_results.json) and include full regression tables with SEs and CIs to facilitate comprehensive comparison.

notes: Effect size is attenuated relative to the original and only meets one-tailed significance; recommend sensitivity checks (e.g., robust SEs, alternative codings for gender/children, verifying invariance of standardized results) and replication across additional cohorts. Sample differences (time period and possibly locale) may partly explain attenuation. Listwise deletion reduces N; consider multiple imputation as a robustness check in future work.
